title: What Everyone Sought Was the Release of the Seven!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The lengthy English newspaper passages are reproduced from the printed source scans. OCR corruptions such as `AAADMK`, `Tami]`, `184. Radhakrishnan`, and `ri ght` are restored to the visible readings `AIADMK`, `Tamil`, `M. Radhakrishnan`, and `right`. The 4 March editorial visibly prints `grating freedom`; that source wording is retained rather than silently changed. The Tamil reading copy also restores `அரசியல் சாசன அமர்வு` and `ஏழு பேரையும்`. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: In the assassination case of India's former Prime Minister, the young leader Rajiv Gandhi, the trial court sentenced seven people—Murugan, Santhan, Perarivalan, Nalini, Jayakumar, Ravichandran and Robert Payas—to death. The sentences of Nalini, Jayakumar, Robert Payas and Ravichandran were later commuted to life imprisonment. In proceedings before the Supreme Court seeking the release of all seven, the Court ordered that the death sentences of Murugan, Santhan and Perarivalan also be commuted to life imprisonment.

Speaking about the case, Chief Justice of India P. Sathasivam said: “In our judgment, we have stated only that the death sentences of the three convicts have been commuted to life imprisonment. We said nothing about their release. We made it clear in the judgment that the state government concerned may follow the normal legal procedure. Under that procedure, the convicts must submit a formal petition. The state government must then seek a report from the trial court concerned. Only after that should an appropriate decision be made. It is not our intention that the convicts must not be released. Guidelines exist for everything, and they must be followed. Every state government should know the legal procedure governing the release of convicts.” Justice Sathasivam explained the matter at length. Seen in that light, it appears that our state government alone failed to approach the issue properly in accordance with law.

In its judgment of 2 December 2015, the Supreme Court's Constitution Bench held that, in a case instituted by the Central Bureau of Investigation, the Central government had primacy and authority to decide on the release of the seven Rajiv Gandhi assassination convicts serving life sentences. After that judgment, the AIADMK government took no immediate follow-up action toward their release. Just as it took up the issue for political gain before the previous parliamentary election, the AIADMK government has now had the Chief Secretary write to the Central government, hoping to attract votes because another election is approaching.

In its judgment of 2 December 2015, the Supreme Court's Constitution Bench held that, in a case instituted by the Central Bureau of Investigation, the Central government had primacy and authority to decide on the release of the seven Rajiv Gandhi assassination convicts serving life sentences. After that judgment, the AIADMK government took no immediate follow-up action toward their release. Just as it took up the issue for political gain before the previous parliamentary election, the AIADMK government has now had the Chief Secretary write to the Central government, hoping to attract votes because another election is approaching.

Even that letter was not written in the name of Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, who writes to the Prime Minister almost every day on one issue or another. She had the Chief Secretary write it. Does that not itself reveal how much importance Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has given this vital issue? I do not believe that everything the Tamil Nadu government does must be opposed. The very night I learned that the Chief Secretary had written to the Central government about releasing the seven, I welcomed the step and asked the Centre to release them in accordance with the letter. As an editorial in a Tamil daily observed, the Supreme Court's final judgment merely clarifies a legal complication; lawyers say it contains nothing that completely bars the release of Perarivalan, Santhan, Murugan and the others. The authority to release prisoners convicted in cases filed by Central investigating agencies such as the CBI lies with the Central government, under whose authority the CBI functions. When the relevant government must decide accordingly, the present Supreme Court judgment means that the Central government has authority in this particular case. Its central point is that they must be released with the Centre's consent.

It was the Supreme Court that commuted the death sentences of Murugan, Santhan and Perarivalan to life imprisonment because the President had taken an inordinate time to decide their mercy petitions. It was also the Supreme Court that said the Tamil Nadu government could decide on the release of Nalini, Murugan's wife, Ravichandran, Robert Payas and Jayakumar, who had already spent about twenty years in prison serving life sentences in the same case. On the strength of what the Supreme Court said, the state government passed a resolution in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly in February 2014 to release all seven. Passing that resolution and deciding to release the accused were not wrong. But the Jayalalithaa government did not need to act as though it were imposing a condition on the Central government and ordering it to give consent within a specified number of days. Legal experts say that it was because the Tamil Nadu government expressed its position with such rigid arrogance that the Central government had to appeal to the Supreme Court against the release action. Even now, Nalini has told reporters that she believes Chief Minister Jayalalithaa will soon release them all. That itself makes clear that the Tamil Nadu government is responsible for the delay. The state government, which had delayed filing a reply in the case Nalini brought before the Madras High Court in December 2015, wrote to the Central government only after that interview.
It is not only we who understand the purpose for which the Tamil Nadu government wrote to the Centre. When reporting the news, the English daily *The Hindu* stated it categorically.
On 3 March 2016, *The Hindu* wrote:

> “In political terms, questions are likely to be raised by the Opposition as to why the AIADMK Government did not attempt the Constitutional route of advising the Governor to remit the life sentences under Article 161 of the Constitution (Power of Pardon, Remission etc.). The Constitution Bench had approvingly cited earlier judgments that held that this power of the Governor and the President's corresponding power under Article 72 were constitutional remedies that ‘Will remain untouched’.”

In other words, political circles may ask why the AIADMK government did not follow the constitutional route of advising the Governor to remit the life sentences under Article 161 of the Constitution. The powers of the Governor and the corresponding powers of the President under Article 72 can be used as constitutional remedies in such matters. Those powers remain in the Constitution unchanged.

Instead of continuing to argue and conduct debates over whether the authority to release people who have languished in prison for more than twenty-four years belongs to the Central government, the state government or the courts, I once again ask, on behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, that either the Central government or the Tamil Nadu government under Article 161 issue an order immediately releasing all seven.
It was not only *The Hindu* correspondent who expressed such a view. M. Radhakrishnan, counsel for one of the convicts, Nalini, said:

> “M. Radhakrishnan, a defence lawyer, has termed as a ‘pretence’ the Tamil Nadu Government's letter to the Centre, seeking its views on its decision to free all the convicts in the Rajiv Gandhi assassination case. Mr. Radhakrishnan is the lawyer for Nalini, whose death sentence was commuted to life by the State Government in 2000. > > “The State Government can release all the life convicts today under Article 161 of the Constitution. The power under Article 161 is unfettered. The Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is categorical that this power is untouchable by the court. There is no need to obtain any permission or any views from the Central Government for releasing the life convicts. The State Government is well within its right to release them immediately under Article 161. > > “The letter simply says they are going to seek a review of the Constitution Bench's judgment, which is unnecessary. The State Government is playing with the lives of these seven life convicts. This is highly regrettable.”

Nalini's lawyer Radhakrishnan said that the Tamil Nadu government's letter to the Centre was a pretence. The state government had the authority under Article 161 of the Constitution to release life convicts. The Supreme Court's Constitution Bench had stated emphatically that this authority could not be questioned by the courts. Therefore, neither the Central government's permission nor its opinion was required to release the life convicts. The Tamil Nadu government was playing with the lives of the seven life convicts, which was deeply regrettable.

Echoing this view, Tamil Nadu BJP President Tamilisai Soundararajan said: “The Tamil Nadu government has written to the Central government seeking the release of those accused in the Rajiv Gandhi assassination. The ruling party—the AIADMK—discovers the feelings of Tamils only at election time. They are trying to use Tamil sentiment for the election. This is a drama staged by Jayalalithaa. They had ample time, but did not take this decision then. This is an issue that must be approached with humanity.” Tamil Nadu Congress President E. V. K. S. Elangovan and leaders of several other parties expressed the same view.

The *Times of India* captured Jayalalithaa's underlying motive in this issue under the headline: “Parties will be careful not to play into the hands of AIADMK - With remission move, Jaya may divide Cong. and DMK - Tries to gain Political Dividends.” In other words: “Political parties must be careful not to become trapped by this AIADMK move—Jaya may try to create a division between the Congress and the DMK—and thereby seeks political gain.” The report summed up what Jayalalithaa had in mind through the letter to the Central government.
In its editorial of 4 March 2016, *The Hindu* wrote:

> “The decision of the AIADMK Government in Tamil Nadu to release the seven life convicts is a politically partisan attempt to corner Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's electoral rivals and place the National parties in a spot ahead of the Assembly Elections. It needs a perverse disregard for normative politics to convert the humanitarian issue of grating freedom to prisoners incarcerated for nearly a quarter century into an electoral trump card. The AIADMK regime could have explored the scope for a constitutional remedy such as invoking the Governor's clemency power under Article 161. Instead, it has chosen a legally discredited route for political gains.”

That is, Jayalalithaa approached the issue with narrow political partisanship to corner her political opponents and place the national parties in difficulty. In turning the humanitarian issue of freeing prisoners incarcerated for nearly a quarter of a century into an electoral advantage, normative politics was perversely disregarded. The AIADMK government could have explored using the Governor's clemency power under Article 161 of the Constitution. Instead, Jayalalithaa chose a legally flawed route for political gain. The editorial portrayed her underlying motive clearly and categorically. Similarly, *Dinamani*, in its own editorial, centred its argument on the Biblical words, “Blessed are the merciful.”

Neutral observers should compare two statements. On 23 October 2008, when the DMK was in office, Jayalalithaa said: “Chief Minister Karunanidhi is acting in support of the Liberation Tigers and trying to save Nalini and the other accused in the Rajiv assassination case from the death sentence. Whenever Karunanidhi comes to power, speeches supporting the Liberation Tigers take place openly in Tamil Nadu. He does nothing to stop them. Had I been in office, I would have taken stern action.” But on 19 February 2014, when the AIADMK was in office, I said: “The Tamil Nadu government's order releasing all seven, including Perarivalan, brings happiness. The Central government must quickly give its consent for their release.”

Whatever the motive behind the Tamil Nadu government's letter to the Central government, at least at this very late stage the Centre must approach their release with humanity. Speaking in the Lok Sabha yesterday, Union Home Minister Rajnath Singh gave hope by saying that the Central government was examining the Tamil Nadu government's decision. Instead of continuing to argue and conduct debates over whether the authority to release people who have languished in prison for more than twenty-four years belongs to the Central government, the state government or the courts, I once again ask, on behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, that either the Central government or the Tamil Nadu government under Article 161 issue an order immediately releasing all seven.
With affection,
M. K.
6 March 2016
